section.title: Laws of Compassion
section.sectionRationale: This chapter gathers laws that protect the economically vulnerable, debtors, day laborers, widows, orphans, and foreigners, showing that Israel's remembrance of its own slavery in Egypt must translate into concrete compassion, not mere sentiment.
section.thesis: Because Israel was itself once a vulnerable, oppressed people redeemed by the LORD, this chapter requires concrete compassion toward debtors, day laborers, the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow, refusing to let poverty or vulnerability become an excuse for exploitation.
section.frameNote: Seven movements: the certificate of divorce and its limits; mercy exemptions and warnings for the newly married, the millstone, kidnapping, and skin disease; the dignity of the debtor; fair wages for the hired poor; individual responsibility for one's own sin; justice for the foreigner, fatherless, and widow; and gleaning laws that leave a share of the harvest for the poor.

units.label: A certificate of divorce
units.note: regulating, not commending, an existing practice

verses.text: If a man marries a woman, but she becomes displeasing to him because he finds some indecency in her, he may write her a certificate of divorce, hand it to her, and send her away from his house.
If, after leaving his house, she goes and becomes another man's wife,
and the second man hates her, writes her a certificate of divorce, hands it to her, and sends her away from his house, or if he dies,

then the husband who divorced her first may not remarry her after she has been defiled, for that is an abomination to the LORD. You must not bring sin upon the land that the LORD your God is giving you as an inheritance.

units.label: Mercy exemptions and warnings
units.note: the newlywed, the millstone, kidnapping, skin disease

verses.text: If a man is newly married, he must not be sent to war or be pressed into any duty. For one year he is free to stay at home and bring joy to the wife he has married.
Do not take a pair of millstones or even an upper millstone as security for a debt, because that would be taking one's livelihood as security.
If a man is caught kidnapping one of his Israelite brothers, whether he treats him as a slave or sells him, the kidnapper must die. So you must purge the evil from among you.
In cases of infectious skin diseases, be careful to diligently follow everything the Levitical priests instruct you. Be careful to do as I have commanded them.
Remember what the LORD your God did to Miriam on the journey after you came out of Egypt.

units.label: The dignity of the debtor
units.note: a pledge must not humiliate the poor

verses.text: When you lend anything to your neighbor, do not enter his house to collect {{mashkon|security}}.
You are to stand outside while the man to whom you are lending brings the security out to you.
If he is a poor man, you must not go to sleep with the security in your possession;
be sure to return it to him by sunset, so that he may sleep in his own cloak and bless you, and this will be credited to you as {{tzedaqah|righteousness}} before the LORD your God.

units.label: Fair wages, not oppression
units.note: the hired hand who is poor and needy

verses.text: Do not oppress a hired hand who is poor and needy, whether he is a brother or a foreigner residing in one of your towns.
You are to pay his wages each day before sunset, because he is poor and depends on them. Otherwise he may cry out to the LORD against you, and you will be guilty of sin.

units.label: Each bears his own guilt
units.note: no punishing children for a father's sin, or the reverse

verses.text: Fathers shall not be put to death for their children, nor children for their fathers; each is to die for his own sin.

units.label: Justice for the vulnerable
units.note: remember you were slaves in Egypt

verses.text: Do not deny justice to the {{ger|foreigner}} or the fatherless, and do not take a widow's cloak as security.
Remember that you were slaves in Egypt, and the LORD your God redeemed you from that place. Therefore I am commanding you to do this.

units.label: Gleaning for the poor
units.note: a share of every harvest left behind

verses.text: If you are harvesting in your field and forget a sheaf there, do not go back to get it. It is to be left for the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow, so that the LORD your God may bless you in all the work of your hands.
When you beat the olives from your trees, you must not go over the branches again. What remains will be for the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow.
When you gather the grapes of your vineyard, you must not go over the vines again. What remains will be for the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow.
Remember that you were slaves in the land of Egypt. Therefore I am commanding you to do this.

mashkon.gloss: Pledge, security taken for a loan. The law protects the poor debtor's dignity and basic needs, requiring the pledge be returned by nightfall if he needs it to sleep.

tzedaqah.gloss: Righteousness. Returning a poor man's cloak pledge by nightfall 'will be credited to you as righteousness before the LORD your God,' tying practical, costly compassion directly to righteousness before God.

ger.gloss: Sojourner, resident foreigner. Paired repeatedly with the fatherless and the widow as among the most vulnerable in Israel's society, whom the LORD commands His people to actively provide for and defend.

basic.context: This chapter moves through a series of laws protecting the vulnerable: limits on divorce and remarriage, a year's exemption from military and civic duty for a newly married man, protection of a poor debtor's dignity and basic needs, prompt fair wages for the hired poor, individual rather than family guilt, justice for the foreigner, fatherless, and widow, and gleaning laws that leave part of every harvest for the poor, all grounded in Israel's own memory of slavery in Egypt.
basic.hermeneutics: These specific old covenant provisions, the divorce certificate, security pledges, gleaning, describe Israel's particular economic and legal setting, not a direct legal code for today, yet the repeated command to 'remember that you were slaves in Egypt' as the ground for compassion carries forward as an enduring principle for how God's redeemed people are to treat the vulnerable.
basic.originalLanguages: The law protects the poor debtor's mashkon, pledge or security, requiring its return by nightfall if he needs it to sleep, calling this act of compassion tzedaqah, righteousness, before the LORD. The recurring triad of the ger, foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow names Israel's most vulnerable, who are to receive active justice, not neglect.
basic.typology: Israel's gleaning laws, deliberately leaving a share of the harvest unharvested for the poor, become the very provision that brings Ruth the Moabite widow into Boaz's field and, eventually, into the line of King David and of Christ.
basic.christ: Ruth 2 shows the gleaning law of this chapter working exactly as intended: a poor foreign widow finds provision and, ultimately, redemption through Boaz, a picture of Christ our kinsman-redeemer who gathers in the outsider and the destitute.
basic.god: God grounds Israel's compassion not in sentiment but in memory: 'remember that you were slaves in Egypt, and the LORD your God redeemed you from that place,' verses 18 and 22. His own redeeming character becomes the reason His people must show mercy to those as vulnerable as they once were.
basic.crossReferences: Ruth 2:1-23 shows gleaning law in action for a poor Moabite widow. Matthew 19:8 has Jesus explain that Moses permitted divorce because of hardness of heart, not as God's original design. Leviticus 19:9-10 gives the parallel gleaning command. James 5:4 warns against withholding a laborer's wages.
basic.oneStory: The compassion commanded here, rooted in Israel's own redemption from slavery, anticipates the far greater compassion of Christ, who gathers in the foreigner, the widow, and the poor, and redeems His people not from Egypt but from sin itself.

context.literary: This chapter continues the sequence of practical case law following chapters 21-23, focused specifically on protecting the economically and socially vulnerable within Israel's community.
context.historical: In the ancient world, debtors, day laborers, and widows without male protection were easily exploited; these laws set Israel apart from its neighbors by requiring active, structural provision for the poor rather than mere private charity.
context.canonical: The book of Ruth becomes a living illustration of this chapter's gleaning law, showing exactly the kind of mercy and redemption these statutes were meant to enable.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ensure that Israel's economic and social life reflected the LORD's own redeeming character, protecting debtors, laborers, and the most vulnerable, foreigner, fatherless, widow, because Israel itself had once been vulnerable and enslaved.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific provisions, the divorce certificate procedure, exact gleaning boundaries, describe Israel's particular ancient economy and legal system; the underlying principle, that a people redeemed by God must show concrete, structural compassion to the vulnerable, remains a standing call on God's people in every era.

debates.question: How should Christians understand the divorce certificate law of verses 1-4 in light of Jesus' teaching?

views.label: A concession regulating an already-existing practice
views.case: Jesus explicitly says in Matthew 19:8 that Moses permitted divorce 'because your hearts were hard,' not because it reflected God's original design in creation; the law here limits and regulates divorce's abuse rather than commanding or endorsing it.

views.label: A law protecting the divorced woman from further exploitation
views.case: The main concern of the passage is not a general theology of divorce but forbidding remarriage to the first husband if the woman had married another in between, a specific protection against a particular abuse, likely guarding against profiteering from repeated divorce and remarriage.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree this text regulates a difficult, already-existing reality rather than establishing or commending divorce, and neither treats it as God's design for marriage.

study.originalLanguages: The law protects the poor debtor's mashkon, pledge or security, verse 10, requiring its return by nightfall, calling this act of compassion tzedaqah, righteousness, verse 13, before the LORD. The recurring triad of the ger, foreigner, verse 17, the fatherless, and the widow names Israel's most vulnerable, who are to receive active justice, not neglect.

typology.type: Gleaning left for the poor, foreigner, and widow
typology.fulfillment: Ruth the Moabite widow gleans in Boaz's field and finds redemption, prefiguring Christ, our kinsman-redeemer who gathers in the destitute and the outsider.

typology.type: Prompt payment of a poor laborer's wages
typology.fulfillment: James 5:4 warns that withheld wages cry out to the Lord of hosts, showing this principle carries into New Testament ethics.

study.christ: Ruth 2 shows the gleaning law of this chapter working exactly as intended: a poor foreign widow finds provision and, ultimately, redemption through Boaz, a living picture of Christ our kinsman-redeemer who gathers in the outsider and the destitute and makes them His own.
study.god: God grounds Israel's compassion not in sentiment but in memory: 'remember that you were slaves in Egypt, and the LORD your God redeemed you from that place,' verses 18 and 22. His own redeeming character becomes the reason His people must show mercy to those as vulnerable as they once were.

crossReferences.note: Shows the gleaning law in action for a poor Moabite widow.
Jesus explains that Moses permitted divorce because of hardness of heart, not as God's original design.
Warns against withholding a laborer's wages.

study.oneStory: The compassion commanded here, rooted in Israel's own redemption from slavery, anticipates the far greater compassion of Christ, who gathers in the foreigner, the widow, and the poor, and redeems His people not from Egypt but from sin itself.

quiz.q: What is the main concern of the divorce law in verses 1-4?

options.text: It forbids a man from remarrying his former wife after she has married and been divorced from, or widowed by, another man.
options.note: Verses 2-4 state this specific prohibition explicitly.

options.text: It commands every marriage to end in divorce eventually.
options.note: The text regulates an existing practice; it does not command divorce.

options.text: It requires a public trial before any divorce.
options.note: The text describes a certificate procedure, not a trial requirement.

options.text: The chapter has no teaching on divorce.
options.note: Verses 1-4 address it directly.

quiz.q: Why does the law forbid taking a millstone as security for a debt, verse 6?

options.text: Because that would be taking a person's livelihood, the means of grinding grain to live, as security.
options.note: Verse 6 gives this explicit reason.

options.text: Because millstones were too heavy to carry.
options.note: The stated reason concerns livelihood, not weight or convenience.

options.text: Because millstones were considered ceremonially unclean.
options.note: No such reason is given; the concern is economic survival.

options.text: No reason is given.
options.note: Verse 6 gives an explicit reason.

quiz.q: What must be done for a poor hired worker's wages?

options.text: He must be paid each day before sunset, since he depends on his wages and might otherwise cry out to the LORD against you.
options.note: Verses 14-15 state this explicitly.

options.text: He may be paid whenever is convenient for the employer.
options.note: The text explicitly requires same-day payment before sunset.

options.text: He should be paid once a year.
options.note: The text requires daily payment, not annual.

options.text: No instruction is given about paying workers.
options.note: Verses 14-15 are explicit.

quiz.q: According to verse 16, who bears the guilt for a person's sin?

options.text: Each person is put to death only for his own sin; fathers are not put to death for children, nor children for fathers.
options.note: Verse 16 states this explicitly.

options.text: The whole family bears guilt together for one person's sin.
options.note: Verse 16 explicitly rejects this, requiring individual accountability.

options.text: Only the eldest son bears the guilt of the family.
options.note: Verse 16 specifies each person bears his own guilt, not the eldest alone.

options.text: No teaching on guilt is given here.
options.note: Verse 16 gives explicit teaching.

quiz.q: What does the gleaning law require landowners to leave for the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow?

options.text: A forgotten sheaf, the last of the olives, and the last of the grapes, left deliberately unharvested.
options.note: Verses 19-21 state this explicitly for grain, olives, and grapes.

options.text: Nothing; the entire harvest belongs to the landowner.
options.note: Verses 19-21 explicitly require leaving a portion.

options.text: Only money, not any actual produce.
options.note: The text specifies leftover produce, not a cash provision.

options.text: The chapter gives no gleaning instructions.
options.note: Verses 19-22 give explicit gleaning instructions.

questions: Israel was told to remember its own slavery in Egypt as the reason for treating the vulnerable with compassion. What has God rescued you from that should shape how you treat someone weaker or more vulnerable than you?

A poor debtor's basic dignity, his cloak to sleep in, his livelihood, was protected even in the process of collecting what he owed. Where might you need to show more concern for a person's dignity even while holding them accountable?
Workers had to be paid promptly because they depended on their wages that day. Where do you need to be more prompt or fair in what you owe someone, whether money, time, or a promise?

The gleaning law deliberately left provision for the poor built into the ordinary rhythm of the harvest. What would it look like for you to build margin into your own life or resources for someone in need, rather than treating generosity as an afterthought?

Ruth, a foreign widow, was cared for exactly as this law intended, and became an ancestor of Christ. Where do you see God using ordinary obedience to His commands to accomplish something far bigger than you can see?

translationNotes.note: Jesus clarifies in Matthew 19:8 that this provision was a concession to hardness of heart regulating an existing practice, not a commendation of divorce or a statement of God's original design for marriage in Genesis 2:24.
'Millstone' refers to the stone used daily to grind grain into flour; taking it as loan security would deprive a poor family of the means to make their own bread, hence the explicit ban.

The gleaning law does not specify an exact percentage to leave; it functions instead as a built-in, ongoing structure of provision for the poor within Israel's agricultural economy, illustrated directly in the book of Ruth.
